dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for Sick Person.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Good morning. I woke up with swollen lips and a tongue, it made it hard to swallow.
I see. Is it painful?
Yes, a bit. And I can't speak clearly right now.
Sir, can you tell me your name?
John.
You're in Richmond County, correct?
Yes, Richmond County.
What's today's date?
July 21st, 2020.
Why did you call 911?
I called 911 because my lips and tongue keep swelling.
Your eyes are spontaneously open without stimulation. You're alert.
You respond appropriately and are oriented. You know your name, date, and reason for calling.
You follow commands and respond to movement. Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and event consistent with a GCS of 15.
Sir, I'm going to shine a light to check your airway. Can I?
Go ahead, I'm okay.
You have swelling on your tongue, but your airway is patent. You're breathing fine through your nose and mouth. Your affect is calm and alert.
No stridor or signs of distress. Regular respirations with normal chest rise and fall. Breath sounds are bilateral, clear, and equal-no crackles or wheezes noted.
Pulse is strong and equal in both arms. Skin is warm, dry, normal color, no bleeding. Cap refill less than 2 seconds. No edema or swelling in extremities.
When did the swelling first show up on your lips and tongue?
Around 10AM, maybe, and it got worse since then.
Did anything unusual happen before the swelling started?
No. Just woke up with this.
How would you describe the pain or discomfort?
Dull. It also feels tight.
On a scale from 0 to 10, with 10 as the worst, how would you rate the pain right now?
About a 4.
Pulse is 86 bpm, regular.
Respirations are 14, regular depth and rhythm.
Blood pressure is 134 over 96 mmHg.
Blood sugar is high at 477 mg/dL.
Oxygen saturation is 99% on room air.
EKG is in sinus rhythm with no abnormalities noted.
Do you have any known drug allergies or reactions?
No known drug allergies.
What medications are you currently taking?
Lisinopril for my hypertension, plus metformin, and Albuterol when I need it.
Do you have any medical conditions like diabetes or asthma?
Yes, I have type 2 diabetes and moderate asthma.
What did you last eat or drink before the swelling started?
I had breakfast around 8 am.
Have you used new products, changed routines, or eaten anything unusual recently?
No. Everything has been the same recently.
Skin is warm, dry, with no rashes, bite marks, or signs of trauma noted.
Eyes are equal and reactive. Normocephalic. Pupils are 3 mm, PERRL. No discharge or eye pain.
Clear and equal breath sounds on both sides with no signs of rales or wheezes.
Arms and legs have normal movement and strength-coordination is intact. Positive motor strength bilaterally.
Cardiac monitor is in place.
4-lead ECG monitoring has been successfully started.
I will now set up an IV line in your left arm.
We're going to insert an IV in your left arm; is that okay with you?
Yes, go ahead.
IV placed successfully in left arm.
Administering 10 mg dexamethasone IV now.
We'll follow up with 50 mg IV Benadryl and a 0.3 mg IM Epi injection to manage the swelling.
Pulse dropped slightly to 90 after medication administration.
Respiratory rate is 19 now, still regular.
Blood pressure is stable at 132/85 mmHg.
Oxygen saturation improved to 100% on ambient air. That's good news.
EKG still shows normal sinus rhythm.

We are following the protocol for allergic reaction/anaphylaxis. No chest pain, no shortness of breath, no fever, chills, or recent illness. You're alert and oriented (AOx4), GCS 15. No N/V/D, abdominal tenderness, or back pain. No recent dental procedures or contact with anyone suspected of having COVID-19. No headache reported.
Pulse is now 80. Resp rate increased to 23 but remains acceptable.
Blood pressure is 123 over 84. Still within stable range.
Oxygen saturation is holding at 97% on room air. Continuing improvement.
You're being moved to a semi-Fowler's position for comfort and ease of breathing during transport.
Shoulder and lap safety belts have been applied to secure you during transport.
Where would you prefer to be taken?
VCU Health Systems.
We've notified the hospital of your arrival. VCU Health is prepared to receive you in ED-R Bed 8.
Face shows apparent swelling, particularly in the lips and tongue. No generalized skin changes or abnormal turgor.
Rechecking for signs of work of breathing-no use of accessory muscles or nasal flaring observed.
Breath sounds remain clear and equal, no rales or wheezing. BBS are bilaterally intact with no asymmetry.
For cervical spine - no significant deformities, angulation, deformity, bruises, or abrasions observed. No JVD. No difficulties in swallowing.
Have you had any recent dental procedures or trauma to your face or mouth?
No dental work or trauma recently.
In both legs, gait is unremarkable. Inspection of vertebral column, flank, and buttocks shows no DCAP-BTLS. No abnormal swelling in abdominal or extremities.
Dexamethasone was administered and no adverse reaction observed. We'll proceed with Benadryl and Epi.
The patient is currently in stable condition post-dexamethasone and is likely to tolerate Benadryl and Epi.
Any back pain or chest pain with this swelling?
No, it's only my face and throat. I don't feel any chest pain either.
Now administering 50 mg IV Benadryl.
Next will be 0.3 mg IM Epi. This will help further reduce the swelling and symptoms.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how is the pain now?
Still about a 4. The swelling hasn't changed in intensity yet.
VCU Health Systems is the appropriate ECAM for this case.
Any recent use or exposure to new toiletries, food, or medications?
No. All medications and habits have been consistent.
Skin inspection shows no petechiae, purpura, or rashes. No bites or stings. Swelling is localized to face and tongue.
Rechecking neck veins. No JVD observed. No signs of internal or anterior neck masses.
No ear or nose pain reported. No drainage or obstruction at this time.
Rechecking lung fields-no rales or wheezing. Equal breath sounds, no asymmetry or decreasing volume.
EKG still noted to be in sinus rhythm.
Blood pressure is 132/85 mmHg. Pulse is 90. Respiration is 19. Oxygen saturation is 100%.
Administering IM Epi now.
Still reporting pain as 4. No improvement noted yet. Let me monitor that closely.
Keeping you in semi-Fowler's position for the rest of the trip to VCU Health.
Double-checking safety belts and preparing for transport.

All steps completed as per Protocol 3-3 for allergic reaction. No chest pain, abnormal breathing, fever, chills, headache, or any gastric distress reported. No contact with suspected or confirmed COVID-19 cases.
All pre-hospital actions complete. Transporting to VCU Health Systems now.
